sentences.text: Should women be drafted?
This is an effort to figure out if you guys are feminists.
Should women be drafted in the military?
Yeah, so men have to register for the Selective Service.
Currently there's not a draft in the United States.
All men in the United States at 18 in order to be able to vote and have other men have to register for the Selective Service,
which would then make them subject to military conscription in the case that there was a military conflict which required a draft.
Do you think women should be subject to the Selective Service draft?
Absolutely not.
I wouldn't even think, you know, stronger and stuff like that.
Okay, what do you think?
Hold on, so you think they should be drafted based on how they feel?
No.
She's saying if they want to volunteer.
No, just really want to volunteer, yeah.
If they want to volunteer, yes.
Yeah, but then not yet, just for the purpose.
That's what we currently have.
Yeah, I guess we should be drafted.
I mean, plenty of men don't want to.
Plenty of men, plenty of men, plenty of women, doesn't matter.
Now here's the interesting thing, you guys both have things on these.
I still don't know which is actually like the actual feminist position,
because it will have feminists who will argue.
It's actually, we had one last week with Andrew who said,
well, it's not equity for women to be drafted.
That's a whole nother thing.
But what about, no, okay, they should not be drafted,
but they should be paid equally in sporting events, okay?
They shouldn't be drafted.
Hold on.
Okay, so everybody except Anza said women shouldn't be drafted,
but y'all want to be equal?
So wait a sec, how are you equal?
Men are the ones that fight and die to defend the country.
Men are the ones that build and maintain all the infrastructure, right?
Like none of this stuff in the studio built or maintained by men.
You know that like 90% of structure jobs are still held by men,
and the top 20 jobs held by now, 24 versus 1924,
so women have had 100 years to do whatever they want,
and they still do the same 20 jobs they did 100 years ago,
other than shout farm labor.
Other than that, we're secretaries in daycare,
early childhood administrative detail workers,
all the same shit that we were a century ago.
So if the men are the ones tasked with natural disaster,
they have to go out red water,
or the men have to go and fight it and defend the country,
and any time the criminal who needs violently arrest
a man who goes and does that, not a woman,
where does the quality, we're doing the fluffy jobs.
You know, we're all singers.
We got a lot of singers and musicians, which is cool.
It's great.
I love music, too.
But y'all ain't like rescuing people and, you know,
doing the heavy lifting or the dirty or the dangerous jobs.
Like who collects the trash?
You know, who does all the steel working
and oil rig work and all of the like sewerage?
We have a video on this, Rachel, actually.
Nick, remember that video we were looking at yesterday?
I think it might still be on one of the tabs.
Watch this.
No audio.
Go ahead.
Are you ready to work on an oil rig?
All right.
Come on, feminists, get in there.
You can do it.
Those are some roughnecks.
Come on, ladies.
Andrew had to do stuff like that back in the day.
Underwater or welding?
Not underwater, but they would lower tiny little.
I feel like women should just have the choice to do if they want to,
because I know some women that can handle it.
They do have the choice.
They do, and they're not doing it.
They don't want to.
Well, this is the big secret, right?
Is that women don't want to do dirty, dangerous, scary stuff.
They don't want to.
Well, yeah, but is that just, you know,
what we've been raised with, the cultural surroundings?
No, and I have data to back this up.
So if you...
Oh, is there more?
Just let it play.
Yeah, we'll just let it play.
So back around the time of suffrage,
we're talking like 1850s and roughly around there
when the big suffrage movement was happening.
Did you guys know that most women were completely...
By all of the referendums where they would let vote
on whether they wanted to vote,
only about 4% actually had any vote.
They actually had really good reasons for this.
There was much higher membership in anti-suffrage.
These group would debate, do written debates,
newspaper period, back and forth, and debate these things.
The women who were against suffrage said,
look, primary purpose of government, persons in prime,
why task women with that when we're not really capable?
We don't want to have to be drafted.
We don't want to have to do jury.
We don't want to have to be a political bloc
because back then women had things they wanted,
but they weren't a lobbying group.
They didn't have voting rights,
so they weren't able to be politicized.
They felt like they had a moral high ground
to ask for things like we need more party,
clean air, whatever it was.
And that if they became just another voting bloc,
they would be involved in dirty politics.
They felt like somebody has to be morally neutral.
So, given all of that,
do you guys think that women can send persons in property?
I mean, well, first of all,
I want to go back to the fact that for a long time,
women weren't allowed to own property,
or at least not without being married.
And so act on to that.
Married.
Generally, whatever belongs to you belongs to you.
Sure, but then usually the woman is still living with the family.
Right, but why?
So think about why.
Why do you want to live on your own?
Why do you think women didn't own property?
Do you think it was just the patriarchy,
No, I'm not saying that.
and they wanted to move down their neck and keep them down?
I generally don't think that.
I generally think it was just a matter of the times.
Because it was.
So there was a reason.
There was a line not letting women own property.
I think it worked for back then.
I think it worked for what it is at that point in time.
No, I don't think you understand what I'm saying.
So, if you're a woman and you own land and it's 18,
and bandits or cattle robbers or Indians come
and they say, we're taking your land.
But that's exactly my point.
That works for that time.
Okay, what's different now?
We don't have that.
I mean, it depends on the neighborhood, of course.
I mean, it depends on the community, etc.
Okay, so if you own land now, 24,
and you have a squad of squads on your property
and you need them removed.
I did that.
Do you call ladies to come and physically remove them?
Or are there men who come?
So I had a subleaser who I needed to get rid of.
And I did all.
My landlord didn't do anything.
Okay, what did you do?
I went to the court.
And I got the pay.
I took her to court.
I won.
Oh wait, it was a woman squad?
Yeah.
Well, okay.
But did you physically remove her or did cops come?
Well, did she willingly after the judge or she willingly left?
So okay, the lease was up.
She refused to leave.
And so I got the paperwork.
I gave her the three day notice to vacate,
which is the proper way to...
Right, you're going through the eviction process.
Exactly.
And so then, you know, that's not right.
That's totally illegal, even though I got it literally from the courthouse.
And so then we're like, okay, that's going to be a proper eviction.
Mind you, we still had the papers escorted by cops.
So did you both went to the court and there was like the eviction trial?
Yeah, we did.
Well, I mean, what if the eviction trial?
So she didn't leave within the five days of receiving the eviction.
Oh, okay.
Let's say she stayed there, right?
Yeah.
Because I think this is what Rachel is saying.
Let's say she stayed there.
You even won the eviction case.
The judge didn't order.
I don't know.
Typically, eviction, I think they have none.
It depends on the state.
It can be 10 days, 30 days, whatever it is.
And then the cops will come after a certain period of time and then forcibly remove the person.
I think what Rachel's trying to get at is who's going to forcibly remove the person,
whether man or woman.
Even after getting a judgment from court, they still refuse.
Right, okay.
So in this case, because she left within the five days, I didn't have to have the sheriff remove her.
But I mean, at least when I was getting the papers, the cops that were there were men and women.
Yes, primarily men.
But there were women.
I mean, who's to say there can't be more?
Well, you're talking about the process server.
You served her with the...
So it was my mother who served her.
But because of the threats she was making, we had her escorted by the cops.
And that was not like a bunch of strong independent women.
It was a man and a woman.
And if it had just been a woman...
Do you see what I'm getting?
Like, who defends our national borders?
Not a bunch of...
Right.
Use of force is still...
I mean, of course it's when men are taller and bigger.
They're going to have that physical...
Well, I always got it.
I always understood.
I mean, going back to the sports things.
I mean, men are going to be able to do things that women just physically can't.
Right.
So when we talk about equality, the only equality women have is the illusion of equality
granted to them and provided for them by men.
Sure.
I mean, that's why I think it depends on the context when it comes to equality.
If you have to appeal to men and say, men, let us be equal.
We demand equality.
And the men go, fine, we'll enforce equality.
Is that really equal?
Well, that's why I think it depends on how it is being handled.
Personally, I think it's...
So it's up to the...
Basically.
It's always going to...
Well, because they're already in that position.
I mean, if that was...
I mean, if you flip it, then they'd have to ask us, right?
Well, okay.
So you think we could put women on all the oil rigs, women in the Senate, military
as generals, we could make them sheriffs, and that would work out...
Okay, so...
I mean, here's the thing.
I mean...
I mean, okay, I remember watching this one show.
I think it was called Stars and Stripes.
And it took a bunch of celebrities and put them in the position of military.
It had men.
The person who ended up winning was the skier, Pika Boose.
And she was the woman.
She beat out, actually, Sarah Palin.
Who the fuck is that guy?
She ended up beating out Sarah Palin's husband.
And...
This is a reality show.
Wait, where is this coming from?
She ended up winning.
But they were all put in position of military.
She ended up winning.
It was a...
Do you think that men and women are the same?
I don't think they're the same.
I don't.
I got a good guess.
I think physically, there are...
Just physically, though.
Like, you don't think that men have certain strengths, and they each have, like, different
divine roles?
Well, I'm an atheist.
Okay.
Well, not...
Okay, do you think that they have appointed roles in society in order for society
to be mostly...
I think that's how it's come to be.
So, can I ask you another question?
Yeah.
Do you think that women are happier today, or they were happier before all of this?
I think...
Because it's proven that women are way less happier today.
Okay.
But is that just the stats we're looking at now, as in, like, those are the stats?
Yeah.
Because, I mean, for example, like, people like to make the argument that, you know,
we're seeing a rise in autism.
Okay, is it the fact that there's a rise in autism, or did we just not take into
account the fact that, you know, we didn't know what it was?
So, I think people know what happiness was.
I can actually explain my area of study.
There's surveys that were done, like, very large-scale surveys that were done
in the 70s, and in the 90s, and in the early...
So, the first one that came out was called Paradox of Female Happiness, where it surveyed
women all over the West, and asked them what their satisfaction levels were.
A ton of different metrics in the 70s.
Pretty good.
Pretty happy.
Not a lot wrong.
By the time we get to the 90s, and they repeat the survey, women are declining
in happiness in the place where they have the most freedom, the most egalitarian
society.
So, the places where women have the most equality are the places where we see
them not only reporting that they have higher levels of self-unhappiness, but
also, we see a rise in alcoholism, alcohol syndrome, depression, anxiety,
prescription drug use.
Every metric they look at, women are reporting that they are less.
Now, there's a lot of re-talk about why that might be, but I think people
don't understand that when women are trying, it makes life pretty impossible.
You end up having to have it all, do it all, be it all.
Expectations are wildly high, and you expect going to be able to have a
husband, and a family, and a career, and do whatever you want, but also have
a man who's going to take care of stuff.
And these things, all of these things do not, like, mesh together.
On top of that, women are more emotionally volatile.
We have a hormonal cycle every month that, you know, fluctuates.
We have pre-menopause.
We have postpartum.
You know, we have a lot of ups and downs.
Okay, and why does that make us more emotional?
Because, I mean, because, and I'm genuinely asking.
Well, hold on.
I know, I know.
But, I mean, for example, let's look at, like, history here.
So much war has been caused by men.
Well, I have something for this, actually.
Yeah.
So, I mean, they looked at monarchs, for example, kings and queens.
They actually found that queens were just as, or even more likely to wage war than kings.
Oh, I'm obviously agree.
I think there's some real messed up women in history, too.
True.
No, but they did, like, an analysis here.
I'm not just saying there were some queens, just comparing all of them.
I don't know if it was the English monarchs, the British monarchs.
I don't recall exactly the specific details of this analysis they did.
But they did find that queens were more likely to wage war than kings.
Okay.
Just wanted to put that in.
Sure, sure.
I mean, are we looking at it as in, I mean, let's be real.
There were probably still more kings in history than there were ever, like, queens that were really in charge.
But it's perfect per capita.
Okay, so it is per capita.
Okay.
I mean, I can't speak to that personally.
So why would we think that men are going to start more war if women are more emotional and volatile and less rational and less logical on average?
Well, maybe we'd know that if we were taught more about women as well.
So, I mean, personally, I mean, I'm just going to be honest with you.
How many women in history can we actually name?
Well, I mean, talking in that specific position to call.
I mean, I'm an orthodox Christian woman who were in various leadership positions and waged terrific war on their enemies.
Sure.
I mean, it's just usually, I mean, we saw queens that way.
Oh, sorry, sorry.
Well, that would be, I could give you that except that in the modern age, we have a whole bunch more.
For example, when you look at juvenile crime, we were just talking about how men have the monopoly on force.
Yeah.
So men in male prisons, things like that.
Generally, men grow up using physical force.
And when they're little, they do rock and roll with their siblings, with their friends,
and they kind of learn where the boundaries are of how much can I beat up my friend before I go too far and actually cause harm.
So they tend to learn that young and it has an impact.
Women don't have those same experiences.
So what we see that when women do have, for instance,
when you are a female guard in a juvenile facility or a female guard women's prison,
the women guards are far more likely to be abusive and the abuse worse than men.
I have heard that.
They do think it's partially because of our emotional volatility or hormonal fluctuations.
And also just because women tend to more likely to be on prescription psychiatric medications,
have mental illnesses, and just be less used to knowing where the boundary is.
So when women lose their shit and get physical, they tend to escalate to a point that's a lot.
Okay, can I ask them if some of this maybe because, you know,
also I hear a lot that women don't get the resources they need in the medical field.
You mean like mental health wise?
I'm not talking necessarily mental health.
Like for me personally, I took years to get diagnosed with chronic illness.
And there's a lot of that.
And there's also not research in autism.
How does that...
I think I'm talking about a little bit.
I'd like to offer up a bit of a rebuttal.
So I think you're talking perhaps about how there's less testing certain drugs.
Like less things are tested on women.
My rebuttal though, there would be, well, one thing that's a concern is
if you're testing experimental drug on somebody,
if the woman happens to be pregnant but she doesn't know it,
there could be ramifications there when it comes.
Because for example, some medications are not recommended if you're pregnant, for example.
So if you're testing something experimental, the woman doesn't know she's pregnant.
Obviously it could be very rare that, you know, she's happened to be partaking in a trial.
She's pregnant.
That could be one of the justifications for why they're more inclined to test things on women
There's also a component of, well, women's hormones tend to shift a bit more throughout their cycle.
So I think it's better to test on someone who has their hormones at a lower level.
But the other thing is, it's not clear to me if it's some privilege to have like experimental drugs tested on...
That seems like it would be a negative, like more men are so desperate for money and resources,
they're willing to have experimental drugs tested on them.
I think if it was actually the reverse, if it was 100% women where we were testing drugs on,
there would be a huge outcry from feminists saying,
wait, why are we like subjugating women specifically to this, you know, drug treatment?
That seems like...
I get what you're saying.
I mean, I'm close with that.
But, um, yeah, I honestly think it's pretty necessary.
Sure, but I feel like feminists would just argue and be like,
wait, hold on, this is like, look at how, this is unfair.
Like, why are women being tested on women?
You're not going to hear that from me for a second.
Alright, well that's fine.
But, uh, you know, I mean, for example, uh, minor thing, but like, why has...
Okay, uh, recently only when they test like tampons or pads,
it's only now using real menstrual blood rather than like water or like...
But how does...
It's different.
I just wonder how this gets back to what we were debating, which was why women defend persons and property.
Yeah, I'll just head back to that.
If a hurricane takes out California Seaboard, are the women going to get out there
and get the power lines back up and put the sandbags out
and rescue people from the floodwaters and rebuild everything?
I mean, I would certainly hope so.
No, they can't.
I mean, they can't do it.
They can't do it.
